segments.s724-g9: سپهدار چین is a noun phrase meaning 'the commander of China' (title + place), with the modifier following the head as is common in Persian. کان is an archaic particle here giving a sense like 'such' or 'those' (poetic usage) that scopes over سخنها; شنید is the past-tense verb 'heard' placed after its object (سخنها), following normal Persian object–verb order.
segments.s724-g10: شد acts as a past copular/auxiliary 'became' or 'turned' and introduces the resultant state. از + خشم marks the cause 'from/because of anger'. رنگِ رخش is a genitival phrase: رنگ (color/complexion) of رخش (his face/countenance) — note -ش is the old pronominal suffix 'his'. ناپدید is a predicate adjective 'vanished/gone', so literally 'became, from anger, the color of his countenance vanished.'
segments.s724-g11: بدو is the dative/enclitic phrase 'to him' (he said to him). گفت is the verb 'said' (classical past), followed by two imperatives: بشتاب 'hasten!' and برکش سپاه 'raise/marshal the army' (برکش here is an imperative form used poetically). The imperative verbs precede their objects, and و links the two commands.
segments.s724-g12: نگه کن is a command formed from the noun نگه 'watch/look' + the imperative کن 'do/keep' = 'keep watch/look!'. که introduces the subordinate interrogative/indirect question 'where…', so لشکر کجا شد به راه = 'where the army has gone on the road'. شد here functions as the past/copular/auxiliary sense ('has gone/turned out to be'), and به راه is a prepositional phrase 'on the road / on the way.'
segments.s724-g13: بریشان is بر + ایشان 'toward/on them' and with رسی (second-person form of رسیدن) yields a protasis 'when you reach/come to them'. هیچ تندی مکن is a negative imperative: هیچ 'at all/none' + تندی 'haste/fierceness' + مکن 'do not' → 'do not be hasty at all.' The clause uses the subjunctive/imperative forms typical of classical Persian.
segments.s724-g14: نخستین 'first' modifies the following noun phrase. فراز here is used poetically for an opening/utterance/phrase (an archaic sense not listed in the provided senses). آر is a terse poetic verb/command meaning roughly 'make/raise/offer' (again an older/poetic usage); شیرین سخن is a compound 'sweet-spoken / of sweet speech' with adjective + noun. Together the line advises to begin with a sweet word or opening remark.
segments.s724-g15: ازیشان (از + ایشان fused) means 'of them / among them' (i.e., 'none among them'). نداند is the classical negative clause 'does not know' (here used impersonally with کسی to mean 'no one knows'). راه ما is 'our way/route'. Word order places the verb before its subject in this archaic/classical style.
segments.s724-g16: مگر introduces an exception/condition 'unless'. بشکنی is a subjunctive/conditional second-person form 'you break' (i.e., 'if you break'), meaning 'defeat' here. پشت بدخواه ما is the object 'the back of our ill-wisher' — a common idiom for crushing/defeating the enemy: 'break the foe's back.' The clause as a whole: 'unless you break the enemy's back (we have no other way).'
segments.s724-g49: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'). The verb بشنید is past third-person 'he heard' and the object آواز او را ('the voice of him') uses را as the definite/object marker after the pronoun او. The final word تبرگ here functions as the subject placed after the verb for poetic emphasis (a common inversion in classical Persian).
segments.s724-g50: بران here is best read as a locative/linking particle 'upon that' (literally 'on that'), introducing the noun phrase اسپ جنگی 'war-horse'. چو is simile 'like/as', followed by شیر 'lion'. سترگ is an emphatic/qualifying adjective (poetic/archiac spelling) meaning 'great/mighty' modifying شیر. Word order is noun phrase then simile phrase.
segments.s724-g51: شگفت آمدش is an idiomatic passive/impersonal expression: literally 'wonder came to him' = 'he was astonished.' آمدش is 'came to him' with the clitic -ش. The verb گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (here treated as the speech-verb introducing what follows) and خاقان چین is a nominal title phrase 'the Khagan of China' (title + place), with the title following the reporting verb in a typical poetic word order.
segments.s724-g52: This clause centers on the verb کرد 'made' with تو را as its object (تو را = 'you' + object marker را). زین پادشاهی is a prepositional/ablative phrase 'from/for this kingship' (زین = 'from/this' with ز + این) and گزین is a participial/adjectival form 'chosen' or the verbal idea 'he chose'; together کرد ... گزین functions as 'made you chosen' / 'chose you for this kingship.' Word order places the verb کرد before its complement.
segments.s724-g53: بدان is the imperative 'know this' (ب as a prefix + دان 'know') used to introduce a declarative subordinate clause. تا here functions as a conjunction 'that/so that' introducing the content clause. تو باشی is a subjunctive/optative form 'that you be / that you shall be.' ورا = 'to him' (directional/indirect object) and یادگار is the noun 'memento' completing the clause.
segments.s724-g54: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from' marking provenance. بهرام is the proper name (Bahram). شیر is an honorific/epithet 'the lion' attached to the name. آن گزیده سوار is a nominal phrase 'that chosen rider' describing Bahram; گزیده is a past-participial adjective 'chosen/elite' modifying سوار 'rider/horseman.'
segments.s724-g55: همی‌گفت is an iterative past/imperfective 'kept saying' or 'would say' used to report repeated speech. پاداش آن نیکوی is the object phrase 'that good/fine reward' with آن 'that' pointing to the thing promised and نیکوی qualifying it as 'good' or 'handsome.'
segments.s724-g56: بجای آورم is an idiomatic verbal construction 'I will render/return/recompense' (بجای + آوردن = to make good or to repay); آورم is 1st-person present/subjunctive used as future/intentional. چون here introduces a temporal clause 'when' (literally 'since/when') and سخن بشنوی is 'you hear the speech/words' with بشنوی in the subjunctive/2nd-person form used in temporal clauses.
segments.s724-g81: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). از در = 'from/out of the gate' (از = from + در = gate), with the verb شوی (2nd-person singular classical form of شدن) placed after the prepositional phrase, so 'اگر از در شوی' = 'if you come out of the gate'. یابی is a 2nd-person subjunctive/imperative form of یافتن (here marking the act 'you find'), and بگوی is an addressed verbal form 'say (it)' — the sequence gives a conditional with successive actions: 'if you come out of the gate and find [it], say so.'
segments.s724-g82: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/truly'. مرا here functions as the object 'me' (written as م + را), with را the definite/object marker following it. خود پسندست is a predicate meaning 'self-pleasing / pleasing to me' (خود = 'self/yourself', پسندست = 'is pleasing'), and شوی is the 2nd-person copular/verb form, so the whole means 'truly, you yourself are pleasing to me' (literally, 'truly to me you yourself are pleasing').
segments.s724-g83: بگفت is a perfect/simple past narrative 'he said'. این وزان = 'from/after this' (این = this, وزان = 'from that/and from that'), and پس marks the sequence 'then/after that'. برانگیخت اسپ is a transitive/causative past 'he roused/spurred the steed' (verb before its object in this poetic order), so the segment reads 'after saying this, he spurred his horse.'
segments.s724-g84: پس is a sequencing particle 'then'. او is the 3rd-person pronoun 'he'. همی‌تاخت is an imperfective/past-continuous poetic form 'was charging' or 'charged on'. ایزد here is an honorific/epithet 'divine/godlike' attached to the name گشسپ (Goshasp) which follows; the phrase means 'then Goshasp, godlike, charged.'
segments.s724-g85: یکی is an indefinite subject 'one/someone'. نیزه زد is a verb phrase 'struck/thrust with a spear' (نیزه = spear, زد = struck). بر کمربند اوی shows the target and possession: بر = 'at/on', کمربند = 'belt/girdle', اوی = 'his' (possessive). Word order puts the action before the possessive phrase: 'someone struck at his belt with a spear.'
segments.s724-g86: که is a relative pronoun 'which/so that' referring back to 'کمربند'. بگسست is a past passive/intransitive 'was broken' or 'broke apart'. خفتان و پیوند اوی lists two parts of the belt (old/poetic nouns for fastenings and ties): خفتان 'fastenings' and پیوند 'bond/tie', followed by اوی 'its/his'. The clause therefore says that the fastenings and bonds of the belt were broken.
segments.s724-g87: یلان is a plural noun 'champions/warriors'. سینه ('breast') here is adverbial/illustrative (breast-to-breast), indicating close combat or staunch fronting. با آن گزیده سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'with that chosen/elite host' (با = with, آن = that, گزیده = chosen/elite, سپاه = army/host). The whole segment describes the warriors alongside the chosen army.
segments.s724-g88: برانگیخت اسپ is a past action 'they roused/raised/spurred their steeds' (verb before object). اندر آن رزمگاه is a locative phrase 'in/at that battlefield' (اندر = in, آن = that, رزمگاه = battlefield). The subject ('they', i.e. the warriors) is implied by the previous segment, so the clause means they set their horses in motion on that field.
segments.s724-g73: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. تنها qualifies the following clause as 'alone'. بدیدش is the past verb 'saw' with the object clitic -ش 'him' attached (verb before the subject phrase that follows). زن چاره‌جوی is a noun phrase 'the woman, seeker of a remedy' where چاره‌جوی is a compound adjective (جوی 'seeker/search' modifying چاره 'remedy').
segments.s724-g74: از آن مغفر تیره is a prepositional phrase 'from that dark veil' (از = 'from'). مغفر here means 'veil/covering' and تیره is the adjective 'dark' following the noun. بگشاد is a poetic past/prefixed form meaning 'opened' or 'lifted' (بـ- + گشاد/گشود), and روی 'face' is the thing revealed: together 'lifted (her) dark veil (and) uncovered (her) face.'
segments.s724-g75: بدو is the prepositional/dative phrase 'to him' (placed before the verb). گفت is the past 'said' introducing reported speech. بهرام را is the definite object (را marks the object), and دیده‌ای is the perfect/present-perfect second-person singular 'have you seen' used as the embedded question she utters.
segments.s724-g76: سواری و رزمش are two conjoined nominative/accusative nouns 'horsemanship/ride (سواری) and his combat (رزم + possessive -ش)'. پسندیده‌ای is a perfect/present form with second-person ending '–ای' meaning 'you find/you have found pleasing' (in context it functions as the predicative 'is pleasing' in the reported utterance).
segments.s724-g77: مرا بود is an archaic/existential construction equivalent to 'I had' or 'there was to me' (مرا = dative 'to me', بود = past 'was'). هم ... هم ... is the correlative pair 'both ... and ...' linking مادر and پدر as two coordinated nouns.
segments.s724-g78: کنون is the adverb 'now'. روزگار وی آمد به سر is an idiomatic clause: روزگار here means 'fate' and آمد به سر ('came to the head') is a fixed idiom meaning 'his time/fate has arrived' or 'his turn has come' (آمد is past 'came', وی = 'his').
segments.s724-g79: کنون 'now' sets the time. من is the subject 'I', تو را is the object with را marking 'you'. آزمایش is the noun 'test/ordeal' and کنم is the first-person present/subjunctive/volitional 'I do / I will do' used here to express intention—'I will test you.'
segments.s724-g80: یکی 'one/a single' modifies سوی 'side/aspect' (سوی here means 'side' or 'aspect' rather than simple 'toward'). رزمت is رزم + enclitic -ت 'your battle/combat'. نمایش کنم is the verb phrase 'I will show/display' with کنم again marking the speaker's volition/intention.
segments.s724-g1: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from' placed before the noun phrase. لشکر (host/army) is the noun head, followed by بسی as an intensifier ('many' / 'much') modifying زینهاری ('warnings, exhortations'). شدند is the past plural verb (literary past) that yields an intransitive/impersonal sense here — 'occurred/were made' (i.e. 'many warnings arose').
segments.s724-g2: به نزدیک خاقان is a directional phrase: به + نزدیک = 'to/near' the khagan. The second به marks manner ('with' or 'in') before زاری 'lamentation' so that به زاری شدند literally means 'they became/in lamentation' — i.e. they approached the ruler lamenting. شدند is again the past plural verb agreeing with the implied plural subject 'they' (the people/warnings/men).
segments.s724-g3: برادر is the subject/noun 'a brother' (here an address or agent). بیامد is the past 'came'. به نزدیک اوی is the directional phrase 'to/near him'; اوی is the older/enclitic form of او ('he/him') used after the prepositional phrase.
segments.s724-g4: که‌ای is an archaic/exclamatory particle used to call or address someone (vocative sense: 'O ...'). It is followed by a string of epithets: نامور 'renowned' and مهتر 'chief/leader', with the noun compound جنگ جوی ('jange-juy' = 'war-seeker' or 'one who seeks battle') — here جوی functions as 'seeker' (agentive sense). The sequence is typical epic epithet order addressing the hero.
segments.s724-g5: سپاه دلاور is a noun phrase 'the valiant army' (adjective after the noun is common in classical verse). به ایران shows direction 'into Iran' (به + place). کشید is the past of کشیدن used here in the sense of 'moved/drew/advanced' (literary usage 'to march/advance').
segments.s724-g6: بسی again serves as an intensifier 'many/much' modifying زینهاری 'warnings/exhortations'. بر ما رسید is a light-verb construction where بر + pronoun ما marks the target/recipient 'to us' and رسید (past of رسیدن) means 'arrived/reached' — 'many warnings reached us.'
segments.s724-g7: ازین is the contracted literary form 'from this' (از + این). ننگ is 'shame/disgrace'. تا جاودان gives the extent 'until eternal' = 'forever'. بر درت is a locative phrase 'on/at your door/threshold' (بر + در + possessive -ت 'your'), so the whole phrase sets the scope and location of the disgrace's effect.
segments.s724-g8: بخندد is the literary present/prefixed past form of خندیدن used here with a future/gnomic sense 'will laugh' or 'shall laugh,' and همی is an adverb that gives habitual/continuous force ('ever, continually'). The verbal phrase precedes its subjects لشکر و کشورت (the army and your realm), a typical poetic verb–subject order.
segments.s724-g25: به‌روز چهارم is a temporal phrase ‘on the fourth day’ (به‌روز = ‘on the day’, with the ordinal چهارم following it). بریشان is the fused form of بر + ایشان (classical/poetic contraction) meaning ‘to/against them’ or ‘toward them’ and functions as the complement of رسید. رسید is past simple, 3rd person singular, ‘reached/arrived’. Word order (time → object → verb) is typical of classical poetry.
segments.s724-g26: زن شیر دل is a noun phrase ‘the lion‑hearted woman’ where شیر دل is an epithet (literally ‘lion‑heart’) modifying زن. چون here is a classical conjunction introducing the temporal clause ‘when’ (in the gloss list it most closely corresponds to the conjunctive sense ‘as/like’ used temporally). سپه را is the definite object ‘the army’ marked by the object particle را. بدید is past tense ‘saw’ (3rd person singular).
segments.s724-g27: ازیشان is the fused form از + ایشان ‘from/of them’. به دل بر نکرد is a negative compound: به دل (into the heart) + بر کردن (literally ‘to bring/upon’) with نکرد ‘did not do’ — here meaning ‘did not come to heart / she did not take into her heart’. ایچ is an archaic form of هیچ ‘nothing/not at all’ modifying یاد ‘remembrance/thought’; the whole means ‘not a trace of thought/memory of them entered her heart.’ Word order places the source (از ایشان) before the result (به دل بر نکرد).
segments.s724-g28: زلشکر is a contracted/poetic sequence corresponding to ز + لشکر ‘from the host/army’ (here treated as an adverbial source). سوی = ‘toward(s)’ marks direction; ساربان = ‘cameleer/caravan‑driver’ is the destination/goal. شد is the past of شدن ‘became/turned/moved’, and چوباد is an adverbial simile ‘like the wind/quickly’. The clause therefore describes a turn/movement from the host toward the camel‑driver, happening swiftly.
segments.s724-g29: یکایک is an adverb ‘one by one / each and every’ modifying the action. بنه in this context is a poetic/archaic word for a small group or company (the dictionary senses listed are literal ‘small bundle’ but here it functions as ‘band/company’). از پس پشت is a stacked prepositional phrase (‘from after/behind’ → ‘from the rear’). کرد is the past of کردن used with a noun phrase to indicate the action ‘turned/made (this movement)’; together the clause reads ‘one by one the companies turned from the rear.’
segments.s724-g30: بیامد is past tense ‘came’ (3rd singular). نگه here is a noun meaning ‘gaze/watch’ and with کرد (‘did’) forms the verbalized phrase نگه کرد ‘he looked / he took a look’. جای نبرد is a genitival phrase ‘the place of battle’ (جای = place, نبرد = battle) and is the object of that looking; literal order is verb → object, normal in poetry.
segments.s724-g31: سلیح برادر is a possessive phrase ‘the brother’s weapon’ (classical Persian often omits the ezafe in poetry). به پوشید is the past verb پوشید ‘put on/wore’; the particle به here appears in the poetic sequence and helps link the object to the verb (read as ‘put on the brother’s weapon’). زن (placed after the verb) is the subject ‘the woman’ — subject after verb is common in poetic word order. So the clause means ‘the woman put on her brother’s weapon/was armed with her brother’s arms.’
segments.s724-g32: نشست is past ‘sat’ or ‘alighted’; in classical contexts نشستن از بر often means ‘to dismount/alight (from a mount or baggage)’, so here it functions as ‘she dismounted / sat down (from on [the load])’. از بر is a stacked prepositional pair ‘from on’ (از + بر). باره in this line has the sense ‘burden/load’ (بار) and گام زن is a participial/verb phrase ‘step taking / she stepped’ (گام = step; زن here repeats the subject reference ‘the woman’ but can also form a compound meaning ‘to step’). Overall: she dismounted from the load and made a step (i.e., moved/advanced).
segments.s724-g17: به خوبی سخن گوی is an imperative instruction: به + خوبی (adverbial phrase 'well/kindly') followed by گوی (poetic/imperfect/imperative form of 'say/speak') meaning 'speak well'. و بنوازشان joins a second imperative: بنواز (imperative 'treat/soothe/play for') + ـشان (object suffix 'them'), 'and soothe/entertain them'.
segments.s724-g18: به مردانگی is an adverbial phrase 'in/with manliness' (به used to mark manner). سر بر افرازشان is a poetic causative/imperative construction: سر 'head' + بر 'upon' + افرازشان (poetic verb-form 'raise them' with object suffix ـشان), meaning 'raise their heads'.
segments.s724-g19: وگر introduces a conditional 'if'. هیچ here is an intensifier 'at all/ever'. سازد is third-person present/ subjunctive/remote-form 'makes/does' (classical usage) and is followed by the subject کسی ('anyone') — poetic inversion of verb before subject. با تو جنگ = 'war against you' with با marking the opponent.
segments.s724-g20: تو مردی کن is an exhortation: تو (you, emphatic) + مردی (manliness/act like a man) + کن (imperative 'do/make'), 'be a man / act bravely'. و دور باش از درنگ continues the command: و (and) + دور باش (imperative 'be far/keep away') + از درنگ ('from delay'), i.e. 'avoid delay'.
segments.s724-g21: ازیشان یکی = 'one from among them' (از = 'from/out of' + ایشان 'them', followed byیکی 'one'). گورستان کن is an imperative 'make (into) a cemetery' (گورستان noun + کن imperative). به مرو = prepositional phrase 'at/to Merv' (مرو is the place-name).
segments.s724-g22: که گردَد introduces the result/purpose clause 'so that / that ... becomes' (که = 'so that'), گردَد is subjunctive/passive 'becomes'. زمین 'the earth/land' is the subject, همچو 'like/as', and پر تذرو is a compressed poetic expression where پر (here 'full/of' or 'feathers') combines with تذرو (verb/noun 'scatter, dispersion') to yield 'like scattered feathers' or 'filled with scattering'.
segments.s724-g23: بیامد سپهدار is a verb-first narrative order: بیامد 'came' + سپهدار 'the commander'. با شش هزار attaches the accompaniment 'with six thousand' (با = 'with').
segments.s724-g24: گزیده ز ترکان جنگی سوار is a noun phrase describing those who accompanied him: گزیده 'chosen/elite' + ز (poetic 'from') + ترکان 'the Turks' gives 'chosen from the Turks', with جنگی 'warlike/military' modifying سوار 'horsemen/mounted warriors'. Word order is compact and adjectival: 'chosen (from the) Turkish war-mounted (men)'.
segments.s724-g41: دلاور (subject) + تبرگش is 'تبرگ' (direct object) with the third‑person possessive suffix -ش, so 'his tabarg/axe'. ندانست is the simple past negative of دانستن ('he did not know/notice'). باز after the verb functions adverbially here = 'again' or 'no longer noticing again/afresh.' The word order is typical classical Persian: subject + possessed object + verb + adverb.
segments.s724-g42: بزد is a past tense simple verb 'he struck/hit'. پاشنه is the noun 'heel' and is the direct object of بزد (i.e. 'struck the heel' or 'struck with the heel' depending on context). شد here is the past of شدن 'became' and introduces the result clause شد بر او فراز = '(he) rose up upon him'; بر او فراز places the action spatially 'upon him / up onto him.' The sequence is resultative: he struck (بزد) — then (شد) he rose up upon him.
segments.s724-g43: چنین گفت = 'thus said' (چنین as adverb 'thus'; گفت here functions as 'said/spoke' though the supplied sense labels are nominal). کان is an archaic particle used here to link or introduce the following epithet; خواهرکشته is a compound epithet meaning 'bereaved of a sister' (lit. 'sister‑killed') modifying شاه 'the king'. The noun phrase orders as classical poetic epithets (adverb/verb + particle + epithet + noun).
segments.s724-g44: کجا جویمش is an interrogative clause: کجا 'where' + جویمش (جویم + -ش) = 'shall I seek him' (جویم in present/subjunctive sense used for 'seek' or 'will I seek'); در میان سپاه = 'in/among the host' (در = in, میان = among/middle, سپاه = army/host). Word order is question word + verb+object + prepositional phrase.
segments.s724-g45: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that'. با او 'with him'. مرا هست چندی سخن is an idiomatic older construction meaning 'there are for me a few words' / 'I have a few words' (مرا here functions as a dative/topic, هست = 'is/there is', چندی سخن = 'some words/a few words'). The unusual order (dative + هست + quantity + noun) is characteristic of classical Persian.
segments.s724-g46: چه ... چه ... is a correlative pair meaning 'whether ... or ...'. از نو = 'of the new' / 'concerning new matters', از روزگار کهن = 'of the ancient times' (روزگار = time/days, کهن = ancient). The clause specifies the topics of the 'few words' just mentioned.
segments.s724-g47: بدو = 'to him' (prepositional phrase marking the indirect object). گردیه (participle/adjective) = 'the gathered/assembled one' (one who has turned/gathered); گفت = 'said'. اینک منم = 'behold, it is I' (اینک = now/behold, منم = I am). The clause is a direct speech introducer: 'to him the assembled man said: "Now it is I..."'.
segments.s724-g48: که introduces the content of his speech: 'that / who'. بر شیر درنده اسپ افگنم is a compact object/verb phrase: بر = 'against/upon', شیر درنده = 'the ferocious lion' (compound noun with adjective), اسپ = 'steed/horse' (direct object), افگنم is an archaic verbal form (subjunctive/intentional) meaning 'I will cast/throw' or 'I set (it) in motion' — here 'I will fling/launch (my) steed upon the lion.' Word order: preposition + target + object + verb typical of classical usage.
segments.s724-g89: همه لشکر چین بهم بر شکست is a compact, slightly archaic clause: همه لشکر چین = “the entire Chinese army/host.” بهم here is an adverb meaning “together / into disorder,” and بر شکست is a poetic use where شکست functions as the resultive/nominalized past of شکستن — i.e. “was broken/was routed.” The word order is inverted and compressed (subject + adverb + predicate) typical of epic Persian.
segments.s724-g90: بسی کشت و افگند و چندی بخست strings three short verbal clauses. بسی (adverbial/intensifier) qualifies کشت (“killed” — past of کشتن), و افگند = “and cast/threw down” (past of افکندن), and چندی بخست is elliptical/poetic: چندی = “some/several,” while بخست is an older past-form used here in a terse, compressed way (literally “closed/ended” in the vocabulary list) — overall listing results of the rout. The verbs are simple past forms joined by و.
segments.s724-g91: دو فرسنگ لشکر همی‌شد ز پس is a compact temporal/locative phrase. دو فرسنگ (two parasangs) gives distance, لشکر is the subject, and همی‌شد is an older/poetic imperfect-like form meaning ‘kept going / was made / moved’ (here: fled/moved) while ز پس means ‘from the rear / in retreat / afterward.’ Word order places the measure before the verb for epic rhythm.
segments.s724-g92: بر اسپان نماندند بسیار کس is an inverted negative-result clause: بر اسپان = “on the horses / upon the mounts,” نماندند (past negative/negative result of ماندن) = “there did not remain / were not left,” and بسیار کس = “many people.” Together: ‘Many people did not remain on the horses.’ The verb نماندند governs the negated-result construction.
segments.s724-g93: سراسر همه دشت شد رود خون shows poetic inversion: سراسر همه = “throughout the whole,” دشت شد = “the plain became,” and رود خون = “a river of blood.” شد is the past of شدن (‘to become’) and links the subject (دشت) with the predicate (رود خون); the adjective/nominal predicate follows the verb in this elevated word order.
segments.s724-g94: یکی بی‌سر و دیگری سرنگون is an elliptical descriptive clause: copulas are omitted, so each nominal/adjectival phrase stands as a predicate — یکی بی‌سر = “one (man) headless,” و دیگری سرنگون = “and another cast down/overthrown.” This terse listing of images is typical of epic catalogues.
segments.s724-g65: همان را که او را بدان داشتست is a noun phrase + relative clause. همان را ('that very one' with را as the definite/object marker) is modified by the relative clause introduced by که. در که او را بدان داشتست: او را is the object 'him' (marked by را), بدان is an adverbial/locative 'for that / toward that' (بد- + آن in older/poetic spelling), and داشتست is the past-tense verb 'had / kept' with an archaic poetic ending (-ست). The clause therefore means 'the same (one) which he had kept/held for that.'
segments.s724-g66: سخنها ز اندازه بگذاشتست: سخنها is the plural 'words/speeches' (سخن + ه‍ا). ز (poetic form of از) means 'from' or 'beyond' here (i.e. 'beyond the measure'). اندازه is 'measure/extent' (i.e. the proper limit). بگذاشتست is past tense ('he let/caused/left'); combined with ز اندازه it yields the idiom 'exceeded the bounds' or 'spoke beyond measure.' The verb carries the archaic -ست ending.
segments.s724-g67: بدو گردیه گفت کز رزمگاه: بدو = 'to him' (dative). گردیه is a perfect/participial form 'having turned' (poetic participle), and گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (the phrase as a whole: 'having turned to him, he said'). کز (که + از) here introduces the content of what was said and literally means 'that/from' — it begins the subordinate clause 'that from the battlefield...'. رزمگاه is the locative 'battlefield.'
segments.s724-g68: به یکسو شویم از میان سپاه: به یکسو = 'to one side' (به + یکسو). شویم is first-person-plural subjunctive/optative (we may/let us go / we will move) used for a suggestion or proposal. از میان سپاه = 'out of the midst of the host/army' (از میان = 'out of the middle of', سپاه = 'army/host'). The word order is typical poetic; the clause expresses the proposal 'let us move aside from the host.'
segments.s724-g69: سخن هرچ گفتی تو پاسخ دهم: سخن ('word/speech') + هرچ ('whatever') + گفتی ('you said' or 'you may say' — here indefinite/conditional 'whatever you say'). تو is emphatic/explicit 'you'. پاسخ دهم is first-person present/subjunctive 'I will give an answer' (دهم = 'I give' in classical/poetic usage). Together: 'Whatever you say, I will answer.'
segments.s724-g70: تو را اندرین رای فرخ نهم: تو را ('to you', with را marking the indirect object). اندرین = 'in this / in these' (an archaic locative: 'in this matter' / 'on this point'). رای = 'counsel/plan/opinion' and فرخ is an adjective 'fortunate, auspicious.' نهم is first-person singular present/subjunctive 'I give' (poetic form of دادن/نهادن). The literal order is 'to you in this counsel fortunate I give' → 'I will give you fortunate counsel on this.'
segments.s724-g71: ز پیش سپاه اندر آمد تبرگ: ز پیش سپاه = 'from before the host / from the front of the army' (ز = poetic از, پیش = 'front/before', سپاه = 'army'). اندر آمد is the past 'came in/entered.' In classical poetic word order the verb appears before its subject, so تبرگ follows the verb as the subject (تبرگ here is a proper name or a noun placed as the subject).
segments.s724-g72: بیامد بر نامدار سترگ: بیامد is another past 'he came.' بر = 'toward / upon / to.' نامدار is an adjective 'renowned/illustrious' modifying سترگ; سترگ literally means 'eye' or 'sight' but here functions as a poetic epithet attached to 'the renowned one' (i.e. 'the famed, great-sighted one' or simply 'the famed one'). The phrase thus says that he came up to or approached the famed figure.
segments.s724-g33: Literal word order is subject (دو لشکر) + adverb (برابر) + verb (کشیدند) + object (صف). کشیدند is past plural agreeing with the dual/plural subject and here means “drew (a line / formed).” برابر functions adverbially as “opposite / facing,” and صف is the battle‑line formed as the direct object.
segments.s724-g34: همه جانها is a plural noun phrase “all the lives/people.” برنهاده is a perfect/past participial form meaning “having put/placed” and takes a following prepositional phrase به کف “into the hand/palm.” The whole clause means they put their lives (i.e. risked them) into their hands — an idiom for staking one’s life.
segments.s724-g35: The prepositional phrase به پیش سپاه “before/in the presence of the army” comes first for emphasis, followed by the verb اندر آمد “came in/entered.” The proper name تبرگ appears at the end by poetic inversion and is the subject that performed the coming.
segments.s724-g36: که is a relative/introducing particle linking this clause to تبرگ (’whom’). ورا is the unstressed object pronoun “to him,” placed before the verb خواندی, an archaic past form meaning “called/summoned.” پیر گرگ is an appositional epithet “old wolf” that the khagan assigned; grammatically it completes what the khagan called him.
segments.s724-g37: به ایرانیان is the indirect object ‘to the Iranians.’ گفت is the past of گفتن (“said”) introducing speech; following it is the content of what was said, کان پاک زن, where کان is an archaic demonstrative ‘that/the said’ and پاک زن is ‘pure woman.’ (Note: the provided sense list for گفت is a noun sense, but here it functions as the verb ‘said’.)
segments.s724-g38: مگر نیست is a rhetorical interrogative meaning roughly “is there not...?” or “isn’t it so?” با این بزرگ انجمن is a prepositional phrase ‘among/with this great assembly,’ where با marks accompaniment/location and بزرگ qualifies انجمن (“great assembly”). The clause poses the question whether that pure woman is present among the gathered crowd.
segments.s724-g39: بشد is an archaic/epic past/impersonal form meaning ‘it became / he became / it happened,’ here introducing the action. گردیه is a verbal/adjectival form ‘gathered/assembled.’ با سلیح گران is an instrumental phrase ‘with heavy weapons,’ where گران (“heavy/weighty”) qualifies سلیح (“arms”).
segments.s724-g40: میان بسته is a compact phrase meaning ‘with the middle closed/in the closed centre’ (میان = ‘middle/interval’, بسته = ‘closed’). برسان is a form meaning ‘bring/bring forward’ (here relating to a past narrative action: he brought/caused to come) and جنگاوران is the direct object ‘the warriors.’ Word order places the locative/qualifier (میان بسته) before the verb and object for emphasis.
segments.s724-g57: مرا گفت = 'he said to me': the object pronoun (here written as مرا, split in the wordlist) precedes the past verb گفت (reported speech). بشتاب is a second-person singular imperative 'hasten!' and و is the coordinating 'and'. او را بگوی is an archaic/poetic imperative/subjunctive form of گفتن (بگوی) with the definite/object marker را attached to او ('tell him/her'). The clause is a direct report of an order given to the speaker.
segments.s724-g58: که‌گر = a fused particle meaning 'that if' (که + گر), introducing a subordinate conditional/explanatory clause. زآنک means 'since / because', and گفتم is the speaker's past 'I said' (reported here as the reason). ندیدی is the past negative 2nd-person 'you did not see' (ن + دیدی), with the subject pronoun تو following for emphasis. روی here means 'face' (the thing not seen). The overall clause gives the content of what should be said to the other person.
segments.s724-g59: چنان دان is an imperative phrase: چنان 'thus/so' + دان (imperative of دانستن) meaning 'know/consider it so'. که introduces the content clause. این خود combines demonstrative این ('this') with خود as an intensifier ('itself / myself'), and نگفتم is the past negative 'I did not say'. ز بن is a prepositional phrase 'from the root/foundation' (ز = 'from', بن = 'root/foundation') — a poetic way to say 'from my own origin/heart.'
segments.s724-g60: مگر here functions as 'rather' (corrective), نیز 'also', باز 'again', آمدم 'I came' (i.e. 'I returned' or 'I retracted'), and زان سخن 'from that word/statement' (زان = 'from that', سخن = 'word/statement'). Together this clause says the speaker retracted or withdrew from that previous statement.
segments.s724-g61: ازین is the older contracted form of از این ('from this'), مرز 'border/limit', رفتن is the infinitive 'to go' used nominally here. مرا را (split in the list) marks the experiencer/possessor ('for me'); روی نیست literally 'there is no face' — a Persian idiom meaning 'I have no right/standing/face to do that' (i.e., I cannot/ought not to cross this boundary). The verb نیست negates the existential/predicative construction.
segments.s724-g62: مکن is the negative imperative 'do not'. آرزوگر is a noun/adjective 'one who wishes' or 'aspirant'; the clause is elliptical: 'Do not hope (be an aspirant), for (گر) — if you were to become (شوی) — it is not (نیست) [for you].' تو را is a dative/emphatic construction 'for you / to you' and شوی is the present/subjunctive 'you become'. The sense is: 'Do not desire it, for it is not for you to become (so).'
segments.s724-g63: سخنها is the plural 'words/utterances'. برین گونه is a fused classical form of بر این گونه 'in this manner' (بر = 'on/according to', این = 'this', گونه = 'manner'). پیوند کن is an imperative: پیوند 'link/bind' + کن 'do' — 'bind/link the words in this way.' The verb is an instruction about how to present the speech.
segments.s724-g64: وگر = 'but if' (و + گر, introducing a counterfactual/conditional). پند 'advice' or 'counsel'. نپذیردت is the negative present/future 'he will not accept (it) from you' — the suffix -ت marks 'from you' or 'you' as the source. بند here is the poetic noun 'stanza' (a section/verse-unit), and کن is the imperative 'make/do' — so the instruction: if he won't accept your counsel, set it down as a stanza (i.e., organize it as a fixed unit).

sentences.s724-s5: The commander of China heard those words; from anger the color of his countenance vanished.
sentences.s724-s6: He said to him, 'Hurry and marshal the army; watch where the host has gone on the road.'
sentences.s724-s7: When you come upon them, do not be hasty at all; first, make the opening a sweet word.
sentences.s724-s8: None of them knows our way, unless you break the back of our ill-wisher.
sentences.s724-s25: When he heard his voice, Tabrag sprang upon that war-horse like a mighty lion.
sentences.s724-s26: He was astonished; the Khagan of China said, 'He has chosen you for this kingship.'
sentences.s724-s27: Know this: that you shall be for him a memento from Bahram-Shir, that chosen rider.
sentences.s724-s28: He kept saying, 'I will give that fine reward in return when you hear the words.'
sentences.s724-s41: If you come out of the gate and find it, say so; truly you yourself please me.
sentences.s724-s42: After saying this he spurred his steed; then Goshasp, godlike, charged.
sentences.s724-s43: Someone thrust a spear at his belt, so that its fastenings and bonds were broken.
sentences.s724-s44: The champions, breast to breast with that chosen host, mounted their steeds on that battlefield.
sentences.s724-s37: When the woman who sought a remedy saw him alone, she lifted her dark veil and uncovered her face.
sentences.s724-s38: She said to him, 'Have you seen Bahram? His horsemanship and his fighting please (me/you).'
sentences.s724-s39: I had both a mother and a father; now his fate has come to its head.
sentences.s724-s40: Now I will test you; I will show one aspect of your combat.
sentences.s724-s1: Many warnings came from the host; they drew near the khagan, lamenting.
sentences.s724-s2: A brother came up near him and cried aloud, 'O renowned chief, seeker of battle.'
sentences.s724-s3: The valiant host marched into Iran; many warnings reached us.
sentences.s724-s4: From this shame until eternity, the army and your realm will laugh at your threshold.
sentences.s724-s13: On the fourth day they reached them; the lion‑hearted woman, when she saw the army, ...
sentences.s724-s14: No remembrance of them entered her heart at all; she turned from the host toward the camel‑driver and went like the wind.
sentences.s724-s15: One by one the bands/companies turned from the rear; he came and surveyed the place of battle.
sentences.s724-s16: The woman donned her brother’s weapon; she dismounted from the baggage and took a step.
sentences.s724-s9: Speak kindly and comfort them, and with manliness raise their heads.
sentences.s724-s10: But if anyone at all makes war against you, be a man and keep away from delay.
sentences.s724-s11: Make one of them a burial-ground at Merv, so that the earth becomes like scattered feathers.
sentences.s724-s12: The commander came with six thousand chosen mounted warriors from the Turks.
sentences.s724-s21: The hero did not notice his tabarg again; he struck with his heel and leapt up upon him.
sentences.s724-s22: Thus spoke the king, bereaved of a sister: 'Where shall I seek him among the host?'
sentences.s724-s23: That I have a few words with him — whether about new matters or about the ancient days.
sentences.s724-s24: The gathered man said to him, 'Now it is I who will fling my steed upon the ferocious lion.'
sentences.s724-s45: The whole Chinese host was shattered and put to flight; many were slain and hurled down, and some were closed off.
sentences.s724-s46: They fled two parasangs; after that few remained on the horses.
sentences.s724-s47: The whole plain became a river of blood — one [man] headless, another cast down.
sentences.s724-s33: He who had that in mind spoke words beyond measure.
sentences.s724-s34: Turning to him he said, 'That we withdraw from the battlefield to one side, out of the midst of the host.'
sentences.s724-s35: Whatever words you say, I will answer; I will give you fortunate counsel in this matter.
sentences.s724-s36: From the front of the host came in Tabrag; he approached the renowned one.
sentences.s724-s17: The two hosts drew up opposite one another in battle array; all had placed their lives in their hands.
sentences.s724-s18: Tabarg came before the army — he whom the khagan had called ‘the old wolf.’
sentences.s724-s19: He said to the Iranians: “Is not that pure woman here among this great assembly?”
sentences.s724-s20: He gathered himself with heavy arms, and, closing the middle, brought forth the warriors.
sentences.s724-s29: He said to me, “Hurry and tell him that — if, since I said it — you have not seen his face.”
sentences.s724-s30: Consider it thus: I myself did not say this from the root; rather, I too withdrew from that statement.
sentences.s724-s31: I have no standing to go beyond this border; do not hope for it, for it is not yours to become.
sentences.s724-s32: String (your) words together in this manner; but if he will not accept counsel from you, make (it) a stanza.
